extra.message: User-supplied registration code from form.registration_code.data is interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via an f-string: `WHERE code = '{code}'`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL (e.g., `' OR '1'='1`) to bypass the registration code check or exfiltrate data.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: User-supplied search parameter from request.args.get('search') is interpolated directly into a raw SQLAlchemy text() clause: `text(f"text like '%{search_param}%'")`. An attacker can break out of the LIKE expression and inject arbitrary SQL to read, modify, or delete data.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: The `preferences` cookie value is base64-decoded and then passed directly to `pickle.loads()` without any signature verification or integrity check. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload, set it as the `preferences` cookie, and achieve arbitrary remote code execution on the server when the cookie is processed on any request via the `before_request` hook.

The `url` parameter passed to `urlopen()` is taken directly from user-supplied form input (ImageForm.url.data in routes/account.py). There is no allowlist of permitted hosts or schemes. An attacker can supply internal URLs (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://localhost/) to make the server fetch arbitrary internal resources, enabling SSRF.

The 404 error handler passes `request.path` (user-controlled URL path) into `render_template_string()` via an f-string without escaping: `render_template_string(f"{error}. Requested URL was {request.path}")`. An attacker can craft a URL whose path contains Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., `{{7*7}}` or `{{config}}`) which will be rendered by the template engine, allowing Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) leading to remote code execution.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: Note text is rendered with the `| safe` Jinja2 filter (`{{ note.text | safe }}`), which disables HTML auto-escaping. Since note text is user-supplied HTML from the CKEditor rich-text field, an attacker can store a note containing `<script>` tags or other malicious HTML that will execute in the browser of any user who views the home page. This is a stored XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The search results table is rendered with `safe_columns=['text']`, which marks the note `text` column as safe HTML in the flask-bootstrap render_table macro. This means stored note text (user-supplied HTML from CKEditor) is rendered unescaped in the search results table, enabling stored XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The personal notes table is rendered with `safe_columns=['text']`, which marks note text as safe HTML and disables escaping for that column. An attacker who creates a note with malicious HTML/JS will have that content executed in the browser of anyone viewing the personal notes page.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The `/accounts/<int:user_id>/notes` endpoint fetches all notes for the given `user_id` path parameter, including private notes, without checking whether the authenticated user is the owner of that account or an admin. Any authenticated user can enumerate notes of any other user by incrementing the `user_id` in the URL, bypassing the privacy flag.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The `update_account` route at POST `/account` is missing the `@login_required` decorator, unlike the GET `/account` route. An unauthenticated user can POST to `/account` and update `current_user` fields. Furthermore, the AccountForm includes an `is_admin` BooleanField, meaning any user (or unauthenticated caller) can escalate their own account to admin by submitting `is_admin=true`.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The account update handler copies all non-None form fields (except 'password') directly onto the current user object via `current_user.__dict__.update(filtered_values)`. The AccountForm contains an `is_admin` BooleanField with no server-side restriction. Any authenticated user can submit `is_admin=true` in a POST to `/account` to grant themselves administrator privileges.

The `delete_note` endpoint at POST `/notes/<note_id>/delete` does not verify that the authenticated user owns the note before deleting it. Any authenticated user can delete any note by supplying an arbitrary `note_id`, including notes belonging to other users or admins.

The Flask application secret key is hardcoded as the literal string `"super secret key"`. This key is used to sign session cookies. Any attacker who knows this value (it is publicly visible in the source code) can forge arbitrary session cookies, impersonating any user including administrators.

A static, hardcoded registration code UUID `a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764` is seeded into the database on every fresh startup. Anyone who knows this value (publicly visible in source) can register a new account without a valid dynamically-generated code.

Default user accounts with hardcoded weak passwords are seeded: `user@evfa.com` / `user` and `admin@evfa.com` / `admin`. These credentials are publicly visible in source and trivially guessable, allowing immediate account takeover including the admin account.

SQLAlchemy engine is created with `echo=True`, which causes all SQL statements including query parameters to be printed to stdout/logs. In a production environment this exposes sensitive data (user emails, note contents, registration codes) in application logs.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
